h1Title: Fungal Toenail Infection (Onychomycosis): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nail plate, nail bed, or both, caused primarily by dermatophytes — most often Trichophyton rubrum and T. mentagrophytes. It is the most common nail disorder worldwide, accounting for roughly 50% of all nail abnormalities and affecting approximately 14% of adults in North America and Europe. Prevalence rises sharply with age: 20% of adults over 60 and up to 50% of adults over 70 are affected. The infection produces thickened, discolored, brittle, and distorted nails that may cause pain with footwear and difficulty with self-care. Left untreated, onychomycosis rarely self-resolves and spreads to adjacent nails and skin. Oral terbinafine — the current first-line treatment — achieves mycological cure in approximately 70-80% of patients with a 12-week course, though complete nail clearance takes 9-12 months after treatment ends.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 14% of adults in North America and Europe (Gupta et al. 2017 meta-analysis); up to 50% of adults over 70 years
keyStats.demographics: Toenail infection 4-7× more common than fingernail; men affected slightly more often than women in most series; prevalence doubles every decade over age 40
keyStats.avgAge: Can affect any age but peak incidence in adults aged 50-70; nail dystrophy in children should prompt cultures to exclude other causes
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300-400 million people affected globally (Sigurgeirsson and Baran 2014)

definition: Onychomycosis (ICD-10: B35.1) is a chronic fungal infection of the nail apparatus caused predominantly by dermatophytes (85-90% of toenail cases), with Candida species and non-dermatophyte molds (NDM) accounting for the remainder. Four clinical subtypes are recognized based on the route and pattern of infection. Distal and lateral subungual onychomycosis (DLSO) — the most common form — begins at the hyponychium where the nail plate separates from the nail bed, progressing proximally with nail-bed hyperkeratosis, onycholysis, and discoloration (yellow-white-brown). Superficial white onychomycosis (SWO) colonizes the nail plate surface, appearing as white friable patches. Proximal subungual onychomycosis (PSO) penetrates the proximal nail fold and is rare in immunocompetent adults — its presence warrants testing for HIV or other immunosuppression. Total dystrophic onychomycosis (TDO) is end-stage disease with complete nail plate destruction. Dermatophytomas — dense fungal colonies within the nail plate — predict treatment failure and require mechanical nail debridement alongside systemic therapy. Diagnosis should always be confirmed by laboratory testing before prescribing systemic antifungals because nail dystrophy has multiple non-fungal causes and treatment is prolonged and not without risk.

typesClassification.description: The most common type (70-80% of cases). Infection enters from the distal free edge or lateral nail groove, causing subungual hyperkeratosis, nail-bed thickening, onycholysis, and yellow-white-brown discoloration advancing proximally.

Fungi colonize the dorsal nail plate surface, appearing as white, chalky patches that are easily scraped off. Usually caused by T. mentagrophytes or non-dermatophyte molds. Responds better to topical therapy than other subtypes.

Fungi enter through the proximal nail fold and grow distally within the nail plate. Rare in immunocompetent adults; common in HIV-positive patients. Proximal nail involvement with white discoloration close to the cuticle is characteristic.

End-stage disease with complete nail plate destruction. May evolve from any subtype or occur de novo in candidal onychomycosis in immunocompromised patients. Requires prolonged systemic therapy plus nail avulsion.

primarySymptoms: Thickening of the nail plate — subungual hyperkeratosis deposits keratin debris under the nail, elevating it above the nail bed and causing increasing thickness, typically to 3-5 mm in established infection.

Discoloration of the nail: yellow, white, brown, or greenish hue depending on the infecting organism and degree of nail-bed involvement. Yellow-brown with a chalky matt surface is most characteristic of dermatophyte DLSO.
Onycholysis — separation of the nail plate from the underlying nail bed — beginning at the distal edge and extending proximally, creating a space filled with crumbling white-yellow debris.
Nail brittleness and crumbling at the distal free edge, resulting in irregular nail margin and nail plate fragmentation.

Distortion of the nail contour — the nail plate thickens unevenly, loses its normal flat-to-slightly-convex curve, and may splay laterally (onychogryphosis-like appearance in severe long-standing cases).
Pain with footwear from a thickened, elevated nail plate pressing against the shoe toebox, particularly problematic in athletic or occupational footwear.
Nail surface appearing rough, dull, or striated rather than the normal smooth, translucent appearance.
Periungual skin involvement — tinea pedis (athlete's foot) typically coexists; fissuring and scaling of the surrounding skin represents the fungal reservoir.
Nail odor from secondary bacterial colonization in the space between the elevated nail plate and nail bed.
earlyWarningSigns: Faint yellow-white discoloration restricted to the distal 2-3 mm of one toenail — the earliest clinical sign of DLSO, often dismissed as normal nail aging.
Scaling and maceration between the fourth and fifth toes (tinea pedis, athlete's foot) in a patient who has not yet noticed nail changes; tinea pedis precedes onychomycosis in 30-40% of cases.
Mild distal onycholysis appearing as a white crescent under the free edge of the nail, distinct from trauma-related onycholysis.
Subungual debris accumulation that cannot be explained by recent trauma or nail injury.
emergencySigns: Spreading cellulitis of the toe or foot with erythema, warmth, and lymphangitis in a patient with onychomycosis — fungal infection breaches the skin barrier enabling bacterial secondary infection, particularly dangerous in patients with diabetes or peripheral vascular disease.

Diabetic foot ulceration adjacent to a dystrophic toenail — active onychomycosis creates a skin barrier defect enabling deep-space foot infection requiring urgent podiatric and infectious disease management.
Systemic fever with cellulitis extending above the ankle in a patient with onychomycosis and compromised immunity — requires urgent hospitalization for IV antibiotics.

causes.description: Dermatophytes account for 85-90% of toenail onychomycosis. Trichophyton rubrum is the single most common cause globally, responsible for approximately 70% of cases. These organisms produce keratinases that degrade the nail keratin as a nutrient source, establishing a persistent infection that the immune system cannot readily clear within the nail plate.

Molds including Fusarium, Acremonium, Aspergillus, and Scopulariopsis brevicaulis cause 3-12% of toenail onychomycosis, usually in already-damaged nails. NDM infections are important to identify because most are resistant to terbinafine and require different antifungal agents.

Candida albicans and parapsilosis more commonly cause fingernail onychomycosis (in patients with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis) than toenail disease. Toenail Candida infection usually indicates immunosuppression.

Dermatophytes thrive in the warm, humid microclimate of enclosed shoes. Occlusive synthetic footwear raises intradigital temperature and humidity, accelerating fungal growth. Swimming pools, communal showers, and locker rooms are major transmission environments.

Dermatophyte conidia shed into the environment from infected individuals persist on floors, showers, and shared footwear for months. Walking barefoot in communal areas, sharing towels, nail clippers, or shoes transmits infection. Household transmission from an infected family member is the most common exposure route in the US.

riskFactors.description: Nail growth rate decreases and periungual circulation declines with age, impairing the natural fungal barrier. Prevalence rises from less than 5% under age 30 to 20% in adults 60-70 and up to 50% in adults over 70 years.

Adults with diabetes have 2.8× the risk of onychomycosis compared with non-diabetics, from peripheral neuropathy, microangiopathy, and immunological impairment. Diabetic onychomycosis is a risk factor for foot ulceration and deep infection.

Athlete's foot is both a risk factor and a reservoir for nail infection. Treating and preventing tinea pedis reduces recurrence of onychomycosis; 30-40% of patients have concomitant tinea pedis when onychomycosis is first diagnosed.

Severe immunosuppression increases risk of PSO (proximal subungual type), Candida, and non-dermatophyte mold onychomycosis. HIV-positive patients have onychomycosis prevalence of 15-40% depending on CD4 count.
Swimming pools, gym showers, and locker-room floors are high-density environments for dermatophyte conidia. Protective footwear (flip-flops) in communal areas reduces exposure substantially.

riskFactors.factor: Trauma to nail or periungual skin
riskFactors.description: Nail injury from sport, tight footwear, or occupational trauma disrupts the seal between the nail plate and nail bed, creating a portal of entry for fungi. Runners and dancers have higher onychomycosis prevalence from repetitive microtrauma.
Reduced digital blood flow impairs local immune surveillance and delivery of systemic antifungals to the nail bed. Onychomycosis is up to 3× more common in patients with peripheral arterial disease.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 represent the highest-prevalence group, with up to 50% affected in population surveys (Gupta et al. 2017).
Men have modestly higher prevalence than women in most studies, attributed to occupational footwear exposure, sports participation, and lower nail care attention.
Patients with diabetes: prevalence approximately 2.8× higher than the general population; management requires closer monitoring due to risk of secondary infection.
Patients with HIV/AIDS: prevalence 15-40% depending on degree of immunosuppression; proximal subungual type is a clinical marker of HIV.
diagnosisOverview: Clinical diagnosis of onychomycosis is insufficient — up to 50% of dystrophic nails have a non-fungal cause (nail psoriasis, trauma, lichen planus, occupational nail changes). Laboratory confirmation is required before prescribing systemic antifungals. The standard diagnostic panel includes direct microscopy with KOH (potassium hydroxide) preparation, fungal culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar, and increasingly, PCR-based identification. Nail sampling is performed by the podiatrist or clinician: the most proximal portion of affected nail is clipped and subungual debris is scraped from the nail bed with a curette. Proximal nail is submitted for KOH smear, while the specimen is also sent for culture. KOH microscopy has sensitivity of 65-80% and returns results within 30 minutes; culture has higher specificity but takes 3-6 weeks and often remains negative due to non-viable organisms in collected samples. Dermatophyte test medium (DTM) provides rapid culture results in 1-2 weeks. PCR (molecular dermatophyte test) is the most sensitive and specific method, returning results in 24-72 hours and identifying species including NDMs; it is increasingly available in specialized laboratories. Dermoscopy of the nail plate surface supports clinical diagnosis without invasive sampling — specific dermoscopic patterns correlate with onychomycosis versus psoriasis versus trauma. SCIO (speckled/spotted pattern) and aurora borealis pattern are characteristic of dermatophyte infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms fungal elements (hyphae, spores) within nail tissue as a rapid first-line test
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician clips the affected nail and scrapes subungual debris with a curette. Specimen is dissolved in KOH and examined under a microscope. Results in 30-60 minutes at the same visit in equipped clinics. Mild nail trimming discomfort only.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific organism and confirms it is viable — essential for distinguishing dermatophytes from NDMs and guiding treatment selection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same nail clipping is placed on culture media. Results take 3-6 weeks. A positive culture confirms active infection; false negatives are common from non-viable organisms.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies fungal species including NDMs with highest sensitivity and specificity; results in 24-72 hours
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Nail clipping sent to a specialist laboratory. No additional collection needed beyond the standard clipping. Cost is higher than culture; not universally available on standard panels.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive pattern recognition to support or refute clinical diagnosis of onychomycosis versus psoriasis, trauma, or lichen planus
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician applies immersion oil or gel to the nail surface and examines it with a handheld dermoscope. Painless, takes 2-3 minutes per nail.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for histopathological confirmation when microscopy and culture are repeatedly negative but clinical suspicion remains high
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch biopsy of the nail plate and bed under local anesthesia. Mild discomfort during injection; results in 5-7 days. Reserved for diagnostic uncertainty.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy is chosen based on pathogen identity, disease severity (number of nails, degree of nail involvement, presence of dermatophytoma), patient comorbidities, and drug interactions. The Scoring Clinical Index for Onychomycosis (SCIO) or Onychomycosis Severity Index (OSI) guides choice between topical and systemic therapy — nails with less than 50% involvement and no matrix involvement may be amenable to topical antifungals alone, while more extensive disease requires systemic treatment. Oral terbinafine (250 mg daily for 12 weeks for toenails) is first-line systemic therapy and achieves mycological cure rates of 70-80% and complete cure rates of 35-50% in clinical trials; it has a superior efficacy profile to oral itraconazole. Itraconazole pulse dosing (200 mg twice daily for one week per month, for 3-4 months) is an alternative, particularly for Candida infections where terbinafine is less effective. Neither drug acts on the existing nail; clinical clearance requires 9-12 months for new nail to grow through. Topical agents — ciclopirox 8% nail lacquer and amorolfine 5% nail lacquer — are appropriate for mild disease (less than 50% nail involvement, no lunula involvement) or as adjunctive therapy with systemic treatment. Efinaconazole 10% solution and tavaborole 5% solution are newer topical FDA-approved options with slightly higher complete cure rates than ciclopirox. Patients with dermatophytoma, complete nail dystrophy, or prior treatment failure require combined mechanical nail debridement (nail avulsion or filing) alongside systemic therapy. Laser treatment (Nd:YAG 1064 nm, CO2) generates short-term mycological response rates of 30-60% in uncontrolled studies but lacks RCT evidence for efficacy superior to standard treatment; it is a reasonable adjunct when systemic antifungals are contraindicated.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks (toenails)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic antifungal for dermatophyte onychomycosis. Achieves fungicidal concentrations in the nail plate through its high lipophilicity and strong nail-keratin binding. Requires liver function tests at baseline; hepatotoxicity risk approximately 1 in 50,000. Drug interactions with CYP2D6 substrates (antidepressants, beta-blockers) require review.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mycological cure rate 70-80%; complete nail clearance 35-50%; superior to itraconazole in head-to-head RCTs for dermatophyte toenail infection

medicalTreatments.name: Oral itraconazole pulse (200 mg twice daily, 1 week per month, 3-4 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to terbinafine; preferred for Candida onychomycosis and NDM infections. Drug interactions are extensive through CYP3A4 inhibition; contraindicated with multiple cardiac medications, statins, and benzodiazepines. Baseline liver function tests required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mycological cure rate 55-65% for toenail dermatophytes; effective for Candida where terbinafine is not; complete cure rate 25-35%

medicalTreatments.name: Ciclopirox 8% nail lacquer (daily application for 6-12 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Topical antifungal applied to the nail plate and surrounding skin daily; weekly filing and removal of built-up lacquer. Penetrates the nail plate via drug diffusion. Suitable for mild DLSO affecting less than 50% nail without lunula involvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete cure rate 5.5-8.5% in placebo-controlled trials; mycological cure approximately 29%; best combined with systemic therapy in moderate-severe disease

medicalTreatments.name: Efinaconazole 10% topical solution (daily for 48 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved triazole topical antifungal applied to the nail and hyponychium once daily. Lower surface tension than ciclopirox improves penetration. Better tolerability and cure rates than ciclopirox in head-to-head studies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete cure rate 15-18% in pivotal RCTs versus 3-5% for vehicle; mycological cure rate 53-55%

medicalTreatments.name: Amorolfine 5% nail lacquer (once or twice weekly for 6-12 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Morpholine antifungal available in Europe; applied once or twice weekly after mechanical filing. Active against dermatophytes and NDMs. Often used in combination with oral terbinafine to improve complete cure rates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete cure rate 38-54% when combined with terbinafine versus 23% for terbinafine alone in a pivotal RCT

surgicalOptions.name: Chemical or mechanical nail avulsion (nail debridement)
surgicalOptions.description: Partial or total nail plate removal using 40% urea paste (chemical avulsion) or mechanical trimming. Used for dermatophytoma-containing nails, grossly thickened TDO, or when drug penetration is impaired. Combined with systemic antifungal therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Nail avulsion combined with systemic terbinafine achieves significantly higher complete cure rates (60-70%) than terbinafine alone (35-50%) in dermatophytoma-containing nails

surgicalOptions.name: Nd:YAG laser (1064 nm) treatment
surgicalOptions.description: Laser energy generates thermal effect within the nail plate, disrupting fungal cell membranes. Typically 3-4 sessions at monthly intervals. Not FDA-cleared as a standalone cure — classified as temporary increase in clear nail. Used as adjunct when systemic antifungals are contraindicated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mycological response rates of 30-60% in uncontrolled case series; RCT evidence insufficient to establish superiority over antifungal pharmacotherapy

alternativeTreatments.name: Vicks VapoRub (thymol) topical application
alternativeTreatments.description: A small pilot study (Derby et al. 2011) found Vicks VapoRub applied to infected toenails daily for 48 weeks produced partial or complete clearance in 56% of participants. Not FDA-approved and evidence is insufficient for recommendation; may be considered by patients who cannot take systemic antifungals.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea tree oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) 100%
alternativeTreatments.description: Antifungal activity demonstrated in vitro against T. rubrum. An RCT comparing tea tree oil with ciclopirox showed equivalent mycological cure rates at 6 months (11-18% complete cure). Evidence insufficient for routine recommendation but may be considered for mild SWO.

whySeeSpecialist: A podiatrist should evaluate nail dystrophy for confirmation of fungal infection before systemic antifungal therapy is prescribed — 50% of nail dystrophy is not fungal. Specialist review is essential for patients with diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, or immunosuppression because onychomycosis management in these groups requires tailored protocols to prevent secondary infection.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatrist or dermatologist with an in-house KOH microscopy or nail PCR testing capability to confirm diagnosis before prescribing. For patients with diabetes or peripheral arterial disease, a podiatrist with experience in the diabetic foot is preferred. Confirm access to nail debridement (chemical or mechanical avulsion) for severe cases.
hospitalCriteria: Podiatry clinic with in-house nail sampling and KOH microscopy or access to rapid PCR testing
Access to oral antifungal prescribing with baseline liver function monitoring
Diabetic foot service for patients with comorbid diabetes
Nail surgery capability for avulsion and debridement procedures
keyFacilities: Community pharmacies for topical antifungal dispensing
preventionStrategies: Wear protective footwear (flip-flops, shower sandals) in communal showers, swimming pools, and locker rooms to avoid walking on contaminated surfaces.
Keep feet and interdigital spaces dry; dry thoroughly between toes after bathing and avoid occlusive synthetic socks.
Treat tinea pedis (athlete's foot) promptly with topical antifungal cream; cured tinea pedis reduces recurrence of onychomycosis after treatment.
Do not share nail clippers, files, towels, or footwear; disinfect metal nail tools with 70% isopropyl alcohol between uses.
Wear well-fitted footwear with adequate toebox depth to prevent nail microtrauma that creates an infection portal.
Inspect toenails monthly; early diagnosis and treatment of the first affected nail prevents spread to adjacent nails.
lifestyleModifications: Apply antifungal powder (miconazole or clotrimazole) to footwear daily during treatment to reduce fungal reservoir in shoes.
Rotate footwear and allow shoes to dry fully between uses; treat existing shoes with ultraviolet shoe sanitizer or discard heavily contaminated footwear.
Take oral antifungal medication at the same time each day with food and complete the full prescribed course; early discontinuation results in high relapse rates.
Trim and file affected nails regularly during treatment to reduce nail thickness, improve topical drug penetration, and avoid footwear pressure.
Wash socks at 60°C or higher to destroy dermatophyte conidia; synthetic socks at lower temperatures may not eliminate infection.
Maintain good glycemic control if diabetic; hyperglycemia impairs both immune response and antifungal drug efficacy.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support nail keratin production during recovery
Zinc-rich foods (red meat, legumes, seeds) to support nail growth and immune function
Biotin from eggs, nuts, and whole grains — small studies suggest biotin supplementation may improve nail quality during fungal treatment
Anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids to support periungual immune response
avoid: Excess alcohol during oral terbinafine or itraconazole treatment, which increases hepatotoxicity risk
Grapefruit juice during itraconazole treatment; grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, raising drug plasma levels unpredictably
High-sugar diet in diabetic patients, which worsens peripheral circulation and impairs antifungal drug delivery to the nail

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and encouraged throughout treatment. Running and sports with closed footwear increase toenail microtrauma; ensure footwear is well-fitted with adequate toebox length (at least 1 cm clearance from the longest toe). Apply antifungal foot powder inside athletic shoes. Post-sport foot washing and thorough drying of interdigital spaces should be routine during treatment.
dailyManagement: Take oral antifungal medication at the same time daily, with food to improve absorption and tolerability.
Apply topical antifungal agent (ciclopirox or efinaconazole) to the nail plate and hyponychium as prescribed each evening after bathing.
File nail plate with a disposable emery board weekly to reduce thickness and improve topical penetration; discard used files.
Apply antifungal foot powder to footwear interior daily.
Inspect all 10 toenails monthly for signs of spread to unaffected nails.
Keep liver function monitoring appointments scheduled by the prescriber during oral antifungal treatment.
prognosis: Onychomycosis is a chronic, relapsing condition. Oral terbinafine achieves mycological cure in 70-80% of patients and complete nail clearance in 35-50%, with new clear nail growing through over 9-12 months after treatment. Recurrence rates are high — 20-25% at 1 year and up to 50% at 5 years — primarily from re-infection from environmental reservoirs (tinea pedis, contaminated footwear, household contacts) rather than true relapse. Patients with diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, or immunosuppression have lower cure rates and higher recurrence. Persistent infection can lead to secondary bacterial cellulitis, particularly in elderly or immunocompromised patients. Complete cure (negative mycology plus normal nail appearance) rates with modern topical agents alone remain disappointing at 5-18%. Combination oral plus topical therapy provides the highest complete cure rates, with some studies reporting 60-70% complete clearance when nail debridement is added. Long-term prevention of recurrence requires sustained attention to foot hygiene and environmental source control.
recoveryTimeline: Toenails grow at 1-2 mm per month; complete nail replacement takes 9-12 months after infection is cleared. Clinical improvement begins 3-4 months into treatment as a band of clear proximal nail grows. Full nail clearance is not expected until 12-18 months after completing a 12-week terbinafine course. Mycological cure (negative KOH and culture) should be confirmed at 3 months post-treatment before declaring success.
complications: Secondary bacterial cellulitis of the toe or foot — dystrophic nails breach the skin barrier and provide an entry point for bacteria, particularly Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes; risk is substantially higher in patients with diabetes.
Diabetic foot ulceration — onychomycosis causes adjacent skin maceration and fissuring that can progress to ulcers with risk of osteomyelitis in diabetic patients with neuropathy.
Spread to adjacent nails — untreated index nail infections spread to all 10 toenails in up to 50% of patients over 5 years, increasing treatment burden significantly.

Ingrown toenail (onychocryptosis) — nail dystrophy and thickening alter nail plate curvature and increase the risk of the lateral nail edge penetrating the periungual tissue, causing pain and secondary infection.
Drug-related hepatotoxicity from oral terbinafine or itraconazole — rare (approximately 1 in 50,000 for terbinafine) but requires monitoring of liver enzymes in high-risk patients.
Psychosocial impact — nail dystrophy causes shame, avoidance of swimming and sandal-wearing, and social withdrawal in 30-40% of affected adults, disproportionate to the physical severity.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association — Fungal Nails
supportResources.description: Patient guide on fungal nail infection, prevention, and finding podiatric care.

supportResources.name: DermNet NZ — Onychomycosis
supportResources.description: Evidence-based dermatology reference with clinical photos, diagnostic criteria, and treatment overview.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Fungal Nail Infection
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient information on symptoms, self-care, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nail Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nail psoriasis causes pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop discoloration, and subungual hyperkeratosis indistinguishable clinically from onychomycosis in 30-50% of cases. Psoriasis requires skin examination for plaques; negative KOH and culture confirm the diagnosis. Misdiagnosis leads to prolonged unnecessary antifungal courses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ingrown Toenail
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ingrown toenail (onychocryptosis) causes focal medial or lateral nail-edge pain with periungual swelling and infection but does not produce the diffuse nail-plate thickening, onycholysis, and discoloration typical of onychomycosis. Both conditions can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (Dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczematous nail changes (pitting, ridging, onycholysis) result from periungual skin inflammation rather than fungal colonization. KOH and culture are negative. Eczema is managed with topical steroids and emollients rather than antifungals.

relatedConditions.name: Ingrown Toenail
relatedConditions.relevance: Nail dystrophy from onychomycosis increases the risk of nail-edge penetration into periungual soft tissue, causing ingrown toenail as a direct complication.

relatedConditions.name: Hallux Valgus (Bunion)
relatedConditions.relevance: Bunion deformity causes shoe-pressure microtrauma to adjacent toenails, increasing their susceptibility to fungal infection.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Foot Ulcer
relatedConditions.relevance: Onychomycosis is a recognized risk factor for diabetic foot ulceration, as periungual skin breakdown from nail dystrophy provides a portal of entry for deep-space infection.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Neuropathy reduces awareness of early nail trauma and infection, delaying diagnosis of onychomycosis in patients with diabetes or other neuropathic conditions.

faqs.question: What does fungal toenail infection look like?
faqs.answer: Fungal toenail infection (onychomycosis) typically causes yellow, white, or brown discoloration of the nail, thickening and lifting of the nail plate from the nail bed (onycholysis), and crumbling or brittleness at the free edge. Subungual debris — a chalky white powder under the nail — is characteristic. One or more toenails are affected, most often the big toe.

faqs.question: How do you get a fungal toenail infection?
faqs.answer: Infection is acquired by walking barefoot on contaminated surfaces (communal showers, pools, locker rooms), sharing nail implements or towels, or from tinea pedis spreading to the nail. The fungi penetrate the nail through the hyponychium or lateral nail folds. Risk is higher with enclosed footwear, nail trauma, and advancing age.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for toenail fungus?
faqs.answer: Oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks is first-line treatment, with mycological cure rates of 70-80%. Itraconazole pulse therapy is an alternative for Candida or terbinafine-resistant cases. Topical agents (efinaconazole, ciclopirox) are reserved for mild disease or as adjuncts. Laboratory confirmation before prescribing systemic antifungals is standard practice.

faqs.question: How long does toenail fungus treatment take?
faqs.answer: Oral terbinafine is taken for 12 weeks, but the nail does not clear during this time. New clear nail must grow through over 9-12 months after completing treatment. Topical-only regimens require 12-18 months of daily application. Clinical improvement — visible clear nail at the proximal edge — begins 3-4 months into treatment.

faqs.question: Can toenail fungus go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Onychomycosis does not resolve without treatment. Without antifungal therapy, the infection persists, spreads to adjacent nails, and worsens over years. In immunocompetent patients, the immune system cannot clear fungal organisms embedded within the nail plate. Spontaneous clearance has been reported rarely in mild superficial white onychomycosis only.

faqs.question: Is toenail fungus contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dermatophytes spread via contaminated surfaces, shared footwear, nail clippers, and towels. Household transmission is the most common route — living with an infected person increases personal risk significantly. Treating the infection and disinfecting shared items reduces household spread. Communal swimming areas and gym showers are also high-risk environments.

faqs.question: Is toenail fungus dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most healthy adults, onychomycosis is a cosmetic and functional problem rather than a medical emergency. In patients with diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, or immunosuppression, however, it is clinically important: nail dystrophy breaks the skin barrier, enabling secondary bacterial infection, cellulitis, and in diabetics, deep-space foot infection.

faqs.question: Can I paint my nails if I have a fungal infection?
faqs.answer: Nail polish is not recommended during active fungal toenail infection. Polish traps moisture against the nail surface, worsening the warm humid environment fungi require. It also conceals clinical signs of treatment response or spread. Wait until confirmed mycological cure before applying cosmetic nail polish.

faqs.question: Does laser treatment cure toenail fungus?
faqs.answer: Laser treatment (Nd:YAG 1064 nm) produces mycological response in 30-60% of treated nails in uncontrolled studies, but randomized controlled trial evidence of superiority over antifungal drugs is lacking. Laser is used as an adjunct when systemic antifungals are contraindicated, not as a first-line alternative. Multiple sessions are typically required.

faqs.question: How do I know if my nail problem is fungal or something else?
faqs.answer: Nail psoriasis, nail trauma, lichen planus, and eczema can look identical to onychomycosis on inspection. Up to 50% of dystrophic nails are not fungal. Diagnosis requires KOH microscopy or fungal culture before treatment. A podiatrist or dermatologist can perform the test at the appointment.

faqs.question: What happens if toenail fungus is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated onychomycosis typically spreads to adjacent toenails over months to years. The nail becomes progressively thicker, more distorted, and painful in footwear. In patients with diabetes or reduced immunity, secondary bacterial cellulitis can develop. The fungal reservoir also sustains tinea pedis on the surrounding skin.

faqs.question: Is terbinafine safe for the liver?
faqs.answer: Serious liver injury from terbinafine is rare — approximately 1 in 50,000 treated patients. Baseline liver function tests are checked before starting. Patients should avoid alcohol during treatment. Symptoms of liver injury (yellow skin, dark urine, abdominal pain, fatigue) should be reported immediately and the drug stopped pending investigation.

faqs.question: Can diabetes make toenail fungus worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetes increases susceptibility to onychomycosis approximately 2.8-fold through peripheral neuropathy, reduced digital circulation, and immune dysfunction. Diabetic patients have lower treatment cure rates and higher recurrence. More importantly, onychomycosis in diabetes creates a skin-barrier breach that increases foot ulcer and cellulitis risk.

faqs.question: How can I prevent toenail fungus from coming back?
faqs.answer: Prevent recurrence by treating tinea pedis promptly, using antifungal foot powder in shoes, wearing protective footwear in communal areas, and not sharing nail implements. After successful treatment, quarterly toenail inspections and immediate treatment of early signs of reinfection significantly reduce long-term recurrence.

faqs.question: Can children get toenail fungus?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is uncommon. Onychomycosis accounts for less than 0.5% of nail disorders in children under 12. Nail dystrophy in children is more likely to represent psoriasis, trauma, or lichen striatus, so laboratory confirmation is especially important before prescribing antifungals in pediatric patients.

metaTitle: Fungal Toenail Infection: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Onychomycosis: yellow, thick, crumbling toenails from dermatophyte infection. Symptoms, causes, terbinafine treatment and prevention reviewed here.
